mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Partnership opportunities with research institutions like the University of Lagos to inform and improve urban development projects

title: NIOB advocates stronger collaboration on sustainable buildings
description:
The Nigerian Institute of Building (NIOB) has called for stronger collaboration among built environment professionals and regulatory agencies

The post NIOB advocates stronger collaboration on sustainable buildings appeared first on The Guardian Nigeria News - Nigeria and World News.
content:
The Nigerian Institute of Building (NIOB) has called for stronger collaboration among built environment professionals and regulatory agencies to promote a more sustainable and resilient building sector in the country.

The Chairman of the NIOB, Lagos State Chapter, Rasheed Owolabi, made the call during a courtesy visit by the institute’s executive members to the management of the Lagos State Building Control Agency (LASBCA) to invite the agency to the 35th Lagos Builders’ Conference, scheduled to hold in October.

The conference, themed: ‘Smart Building Processes in the Digital Age: Procurement, Monitoring, Control and Quality Management for Sustainable Construction’, is expected to bring together industry stakeholders to examine the role of digital technologies in improving construction quality, project delivery and sustainability.

Owolabi said the conference would provide opportunities for continuous professional development for the agency’s builders and technical officers, while facilitating knowledge sharing on emerging trends, innovations, and best practices in the construction industry.

He commended the General Manager of LASBCA, Florence Gbaye, for her leadership and commitment to advancing the agency’s regulatory mandate since assuming office, noting that her efforts have strengthened professionalism and operational efficiency in building control administration.

The institute also lauded LASBCA’s contributions to improving regulation, safety and sustainability within Lagos State’s building sector and the country’s construction industry.

Responding, the General Manager of LASBCA, Florence Gbaye, reaffirmed the agency’s commitment to strengthening collaboration with professional bodies in the built environment.

She observed that the conference theme aligns with the Lagos State Government’s ongoing digital transformation agenda in physical planning and building control, particularly through the recently introduced Electronic Physical Planning Permit System (EPPPS).

According to her, the digital platform enables property owners and developers to process applications for building permits and other statutory approvals seamlessly from their homes or offices.

Gbaye noted that the conference would provide a valuable platform to educate stakeholders and the public on the benefits of technology-driven processes in building approvals, project monitoring and construction management.

“Deliberations at the conference will undoubtedly enhance public awareness and encourage wider adoption of digital solutions across the built environment sector,” she said.

She assured the institute of LASBCA’s full support for the conference and urged the NIOB to sustain its cordial working relationship with the agency.

According to her, the professional expertise and technical contributions of builders remain indispensable to the successful delivery of LASBCA’s mandate to ensure safe, secure, and sustainable building development across Lagos State.
summary: The Nigerian Institute of Building (NIOB) advocates for stronger collaboration among built environment professionals and regulatory agencies to promote sustainable and resilient buildings in Nigeria. This is in line with the Lagos State Government's digital transformation agenda. The 35th Lagos Builders' Conference, scheduled for October, will focus on smart building processes and sustainability.

title: Lagos urges residents to maintain, take ownership of housing estates
description: Lagos urges residents to maintain, take ownership of housing estates  Punch Newspapers
content: Lagos urges residents to maintain, take ownership of housing estates July 11, 2026 10:15 pm Aerial view of the ongoing construction of the Justice Saidu Kawu Court House in Ilorin... Photo: Kwara govt By Adekunle Sulaimon Kindly share this story: The Lagos State Government has called on residents of its housing estates to take greater responsibility for maintenance of their neighbourhoods to preserve infrastructure. The government said that taking such a responsibility would also enhance security and sustain property value. The Permanent Secretary, Lagos State Ministry of Housing, Mr Abdulhafis Toriola, made the call during the ministry’s monthly stakeholders’ engagement with residents of government-owned housing estates. The event was hosted by Millennium Housing Estate, Agbado-Ijaiye, Ojokoro, on Saturday. Toriola said the forum was designed to strengthen collaboration between the government and the residents, while providing a platform to identify challenges and receive feedback for improved estate management. “Wherever you live, you need to be proactive and take ownership so that the estate retains its value. “When you notice something that needs attention, fix what you can. “Where government intervention is required, we will come in and assist. You do not have to wait for government to fix everything,” he said. The permanent secretary commended the residents for maintaining their estates, and urged them to comply with the terms and conditions of their property allocation. He stressed the importance of paying insurance premiums, describing insurance as a critical safeguard for residents and government assets. Toriola also urged the residents to comply with regulations on land use charges and other statutory obligations, saying that defaulters would be dealt with in accordance with the law. According to him, the ministry has continued to organise stakeholders’ engagements to obtain residents’ feedbacks. He said that such feedbacks had informed improvement works in government housing estates. On ageing estates, Toriola said the government had commenced urban renewal initiatives, including redevelopment of the old Jakande Estate in Lekki, after integrity assessment. She said the ministry remained committed to maintaining estate infrastructure, coordinating facility management, ensuring regulatory compliance, managing service charges and promoting sustainable communities. Fosude announced that the ministry had introduced a standardised demand notic
summary: The Lagos State Government urges residents to take ownership of housing estates, maintain infrastructure, and pay insurance premiums to preserve property value. This means that the Lagos market may see improved estate management, enhanced security, and sustained property value. The government has organized 10 stakeholders' engagements to obtain residents' feedbacks.

title: Makinde provides food, essential supplies to military base in Ikoyi-Ile
description: Makinde provides food, essential supplies to military base in Ikoyi-Ile  Tribune Online
content: Add as a preferred source …assures troops of continued support Oyo State Governor, Engineer Seyi Makinde, has provided food items and other essential supplies to military personnel stationed at the newly established military base in Ikoyi-Ile, Oriire Local Government Area of the state, as part of efforts to improve their welfare and appreciate their commitment to securing the area. The military base was established following the abduction of teachers, pupils and other travellers in Oriire Local Government, an incident that raised security concerns in the axis. The abducted victims were recently rescued by the military after weeks in captivity. The intervention by the state government is aimed at supporting the troops who have remained in the community to maintain security and prevent a recurrence of such incidents. Items supplied to the military base included bags of rice, beans, garri, cooking oil, semo, seasonings, beverages, bottled water and other foodstuffs, beddings, alongside basic household consumables intended to ease the daily living conditions of the personnel. Sources at the base said the troops also require additional welfare support, including toilets, potable water, medical supplies and improved communication equipment. ALSO READ: VIDEO: Makinde visits rescued Oriire teachers, pupils in Military hospital The personnel were also said to need operational vehicles, motorcycles for patrols, fuel, security gadgets and improved accommodation to enhance their effectiveness while carrying out their duties in the area. Governor Makinde was said to have approved the delivery of the food supplies as a demonstration of the state’s appreciation for the sacrifices being made by the military in protecting lives and property. The governor has repeatedly reaffirmed his administration’s commitment to supporting security agencies, noting that peace and security remain essential to the state’s economic growth and development. The Millitary personnel commended the gesture, describing it as a morale booster for the troops whose presence has restored confidence among residents following the recent security challenges. They expressed hope that continued collaboration between the Oyo State Government and the military would further strengthen security in the area, sustain the gains recorded in the rescue of the abducted victims and ensure lasting peace across the Oriire axis. title: Trump Threatens to Completely Decimate Iran Over Assassination Plot
description: Sunday Ehigiator United States President, Mr. Donald Trump, has warned that the US would “completely decimate” Iran if the Islamic Republic attempts or succeeds in assassinating him, dramatically escalating tensions
content:
Sunday Ehigiator

United States President, Mr. Donald Trump, has warned that the US would “completely decimate” Iran if the Islamic Republic attempts or succeeds in assassinating him, dramatically escalating tensions between Washington and Tehran despite ongoing diplomatic efforts.
In a post on his Truth Social platform yesterday, Trump declared that the US military had already been placed on alert to launch a massive retaliatory strike against Iran should any assassination attempt be made against him.


“1,000 missiles are locked and loaded and aimed at the Islamic Republic of Iran, with thousands of more to follow immediately, should the Iranian Government act on its threat to assassinate, or attempt to assassinate, the sitting President of the United States of America, in this case, ME!” Trump wrote.


He added: “Orders have already been given, and the U.S. military is ready, willing, and able, for one year, subject to extension, to completely decimate and destroy all areas of Iran.”
Trump’s remarks came despite his earlier agreement to continue negotiations with Iran, although he maintained that the ceasefire between both countries was effectively over.


“The Islamic Republic of Iran has asked us to continue talks. We have agreed to do so, but the United States has stated to them, in no uncertain terms, that the Cease Fire is OVER!” he posted.
Iran, however, rejected Trump’s claims, insisting it had remained committed to the memorandum of understanding signed with Washington last month.


Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Tehran had fulfilled its obligations under the agreement, accusing the United States of violating its own commitments.
“The Islamic Republic of Iran has so far kept its word. Reality check: There can only be mutual compliance,” Araghchi said.
He argued that Washington had breached provisions that required it not to impose new sanctions or deploy additional forces in the region while negotiations continued.


Diplomatic efforts have shown little progress since the signing of the agreement, with only one round of direct talks held in Switzerland alongside indirect negotiations mediated by Qatar.
The latest exchange of threats followed renewed military tensions after strikes this week shook the fragile understanding between both countries and raised fears of another wider regional conflict.


Meanwhile, the confrontation intensified further yesterday as Iran’s Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, vowed revenge for the killing of his father and predecessor, Ali Khamenei, who was reportedly killed during US-Israeli strikes in late February.


In his first public message since assuming leadership, Khamenei declared that retaliation remained inevitable.
“Vengeance is the will of our nation and must inevitably be carried out,” he said.
“This matter depends neither on my personal existence nor on that of other officials. Whether we are present or not, it will come to pass.”
He also disclosed that Iran had compiled a list of individuals it intended to target, further heightening concerns over the deteriorating relationship between the two countries.


Despite the escalating rhetoric, mediators, including Qatar, Oman and Pakistan, continue efforts to revive negotiations and prevent a return to full-scale conflict.


Araghchi arrived in Oman yesterday for talks focused on the future of the Strait of Hormuz, the strategic waterway whose closure during the conflict severely disrupted global oil shipments and remains one of the biggest obstacles to a lasting agreement between Washington and Tehran.
summary: The article reports on US President Donald Trump's threat to decimate Iran over an assassination plot, with 1,000 missiles locked and loaded, which may lead to global economic instability and potentially affect the Lagos real estate market, particularly the demand for properties in areas like Lakowe Crossings and Lakowe Annexe. This could result in a decrease in foreign investment and a slowdown in the market. The article's content does not directly relate to the Lagos market, but its implications on global politics and economy may have a ripple effect.
trending_topics: global politics, economic instability, foreign investment

title: Report: Boko Haram Uses AI to Make Bombs, Plot Operations
description: Linus Aleke in Abuja A report by The New York Times has revealed how Boko Haram terrorists turned to artificial intelligence (AI) for weapons making, including bombs, as well as improving their
content:
Linus Aleke in Abuja

A report by The New York Times has revealed how Boko Haram terrorists turned to artificial intelligence (AI) for weapons making, including bombs, as well as improving their operations generally.

Former insurgents, the report noted, used multiple AI platforms, including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok and DeepSeek.

According to the report, former Boko Haram commanders told terrorism and technology researcher Antonia Juelich of the University of Cambridge that after an assault on a military base was frustrated by a defensive trench, the group consulted AI tools for ideas.

That was when they learned how to modify motorcycles to jump over such obstacles.

Their mechanics altered motorcycles to improve speed and acceleration, and fighters practiced maneuvers before operations.

One of the former Boko Haram commanders told Juelich: “We saw in a movie how motorcycles can jump over bridges.

“We used AI to learn how to do this. We gave it information like which motorcycles we use, the distance we need to jump, and so on. And it gave us steps on what we have to do.”

Another terrorist, a former commander in Islamic State West Africa Province, ISWAP, told Juelich, “You type in the question or use your voice, and it gives you a detailed answer, like ‘How can I build a bomb?’ and then it tells you how.

“It is like a human robot! We used it a lot. Trial-and-error can kill you. AI gives you accuracy,” he said.

“Before, the bomb explosion was not that big, but then they studied it. AI told us what chemicals to put in that made the explosion heavier,” he said.

Reacting to the findings, OpenAI told The New York Times that the terrorists violated its policies with such actions.

On their part, Google and Anthropic said their AI models contained protections designed to refuse dangerous requests.

However, the former insurgents told the researcher that they often bypassed chatbot restrictions by disguising their requests as legitimate projects.
summary: A report by The New York Times reveals that Boko Haram terrorists have used AI to make bombs and improve operations, including modifying motorcycles to jump over obstacles. This raises concerns about the potential misuse of AI in Nigeria. For the Lagos market, this development may lead to increased security measures and a potential shift in focus towards more secure and fortified developments, which could impact the demand for properties like Lakowe Crossings and Lakowe Annexe.
trending_topics: AI,security,terrorism,Nigeria,Lagos

title: New housing law targets affordability — what it means for homebuyers and sellers
description: While the legislation is aimed at least partly at addressing affordability, experts say the benefits of the measure will take time to show up in the market.
content: Bipartisan legislation intended to increase the U.S. housing supply and improve affordability is now law — but experts say homebuyers and sellers shouldn’t expect fast relief. The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act automatically became law on Saturday after President Donald Trump neither signed it nor vetoed it within a set timeframe. The legislation combines dozens of housing measures aimed at encouraging home construction, expanding access to financing and restricting purchases by large institutional investors. The legislation “will help expand the nation’s housing supply by reducing regulatory barriers and encouraging local governments to reform zoning and land-use policies that have limited home building,” said Bill Owens, chairman of the National Association of Home Builders, in a statement after the measure cleared Congress on June 23. The new law arrives as housing affordability remains strained. Home prices are near record highs and 30-year fixed mortgage rates continue to hover above 6.5%. The median price of an existing home in the U.S. reached $440,600 in June, up 49.2% from June 2020, according to data from the National Association of Realtors. There’s also an estimated housing supply deficit of about 4 million homes, according to Realtor.com. “This bill directly targets some of the biggest drivers of housing costs: land-use restrictions, permitting delays, financing constraints and regulatory hurdles,” said Selma Hepp, chief economist at Cotality, a real estate data company. “Unfortunately, homebuyers should not expect immediate relief,” Hepp said, adding that “housing development takes time and many of the benefits would likely materialize gradually rather than overnight.” Among the new law’s many technical and policy changes, several provisions are likely to matter most to consumers. A key provision would prohibit large institutional investors that own at least 350 single-family homes from purchasing additional single-family homes, subject to several exceptions. Those exceptions include certain build-to-rent and renovate-to-rent projects, as well as programs that help renters build credit and eventually purchase homes. Supporters say the measure could help limit competition from large corporate buyers in some housing markets, particularly in parts of the Sun Belt where institutional investors have been blamed for contributing to higher home prices. Economists, however, say institutional investors’ purchase activity remains relatively light even in many of those markets. Another provision aims to reduce barriers to manufactured housing and encourage broader use of factory-built homes, which are often among the least expensive paths to homeownership. Specifically, the bill expands the federal definition of “manufactured home” to include houses that are built without a permanent steel chassis, the metal frame under manufactured and mobile homes that enabled easy transportation with a tow truck. However, few homes are moved after being p
summary: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act has become law in the US, aiming to increase housing supply and improve affordability by reducing regulatory barriers, encouraging local zoning reforms, and restricting large institutional investors. This legislation may have a positive impact on the Lagos real estate market, particularly in terms of increasing housing supply and affordability, which could benefit Mixta's affordable housing projects such as Lakowe Crossings and Lakowe Annexe. The median home price in the US has reached $440,600, up 49.2% from 2020, and there's an estimated 4 million homes deficit.

title: Mshel Hutu Exclusive set to redefine luxury living in Nigeria, says Mshelia
description:
The Group Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Mshel Homes Limited, Arc. Barka Umaru Mshelia has said the newly unveiled Mshel Hutu Exclusive Golf Resort Estate will redefine luxury living and integrated community development in Nigeria. He also noted

The post Mshel Hutu Exclusive set to redefine luxury living in Nigeria, says Mshelia appeared first on The Guardian Nigeria News - Nigeria and World News.
content:
The Group Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Mshel Homes Limited, Arc. Barka Umaru Mshelia has said the newly unveiled Mshel Hutu Exclusive Golf Resort Estate will redefine luxury living and integrated community development in Nigeria.

He also noted that more than 10 blocks of apartment buildings are already under construction for subscribers, reflecting the growing demand for the estate.

Mshelia disclosed this at the commissioning ceremony in Abuja, saying the project was inspired by the company’s vision to transform Nigeria’s real estate landscape through innovative, world-class developments.

Recalling the early stages of the project, he said acquiring the land was a daunting task due to demolition exercises, financial constraints and other challenges.

Mshelia credited the vision and determination of Mshel Homes for turning the ambitious project into reality.

He said: “From inception, the company has been driven by a clear mission: to empower people to own a piece of the world through quality real estate development.

“That vision has guided us every day as we continue to redefine real estate practice in Nigeria. Our journey began with a determination to deliver projects that would stand the test of time. What you are witnessing today is one of the landmark achievements of Mshel Homes.

“I remember vividly when this land first became available. At the time, there were several challenges, including demolition exercises by the Area Council. When we visited the site, my partner called my attention to its enormous potential and suggested that we work together to develop something truly remarkable.

“My first reaction was one of hesitation. The land was vast, and the asking price was intimidating. I did not even have 10 per cent of its value at the time. I honestly doubted our ability to acquire it.”

He explained that the name “Hutu” was derived from the Hausa word meaning “rest,” reflecting the company’s vision of creating a serene community where residents can live, work and relax.

According to him, the estate was conceptualised around the Golf Resort Estate model to establish a new benchmark for luxury residential developments.

He added, “This project did not come without challenges. It came with enormous challenges that nearly kept us out of the market. But when you remain focused, committed and put everything in the hands of God, there is no height you cannot attain.

“We want to create a community where people live happily and enjoy their best lives in Hutu Exclusive. This is only Phase One, and we are coming up with something even bigger than what we have achieved.”

Mshelia commended the Mshel Homes construction team and development partners, including Engineer Evans, for their commitment to delivering the project.

He also appreciated his wife for her unwavering support, noting that positive testimonials from clients, including one from the United Kingdom, have reinforced confidence in the company’s vision.

The company commissioned several world-class facilities at the estate, including a 2.7-kilometre internal road network, a 9-hole golf course, an Olympic-sized swimming pool, a seven-bedroom maisonette, a clubhouse, football pitches, basketball, volleyball, badminton and lawn tennis courts, an estate fountain and a fully equipped sports complex.

“The estate was conceptualised to set a new standard through the Golf Resort Estate model. The idea is to create a resort environment where people can live, play and relax. That is why we invested in facilities such as a nine-hole golf course, 2.7 kilometres of internal roads, an Olympic-sized swimming pool, football pitches, basketball, volleyball, badminton and lawn tennis courts, as well as a comprehensive sports complex,” said Mshelia.

Also speaking at the event, the National Coordinator of the Ministry of Finance Incorporated Real Estate Investment Fund (MREIF), Mr. Sani Yakubu, announced that the Federal Government had raised ₦250 billion under its ₦1 trillion Mortgage and Housing Support Programme, registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission in March 2025.

Yakubu, who is also an Executive Director at the Ministry of Finance Incorporated (MOFI), said the fund had already facilitated 2,000 mortgages across Nigeria, with disbursements averaging ₦10 billion monthly.

He explained that the Ministry participated in the event to celebrate Nigerian entrepreneurship and create awareness of affordable housing finance.

He said: “The interest rate is 9.75 per cent, not 10, not 12, not 15, not 24 or 25 per cent. Applicants are required to make only a 10 per cent equity contribution, and mortgages are available for up to 20.

“The Ministry of Finance Incorporated is here for two reasons. First, to celebrate the excellence of Nigerian entrepreneurship. Secondly, to let Nigerians know that they can walk into any of the 22 participating financial institutions and access affordable mortgage financing.”

According to him, the fund has received a Triple-A rating from Agusto & Co. and a Double-A rating from GCR and has made two investor payouts since inception.

He added that the ₦250 billion raised comprises ₦150 billion contributed by the Federal Government and ₦100 billion invested by 22 participating financial institutions, including 11 commercial banks and 10 mortgage banks.

Yakubu urged prospective homeowners to visit mreif.com.ng to access the platform and take advantage of the affordable mortgage scheme.
summary: The article reports on the unveiling of Mshel Hutu Exclusive Golf Resort Estate, which aims to redefine luxury living in Nigeria, with over 10 blocks of apartment buildings under construction. This development indicates a growing demand for luxury estates in Nigeria, which may impact the Lagos market by increasing competition in the high-end segment. With multiple blocks already under construction, this project may attract potential buyers away from other luxury developments in Lagos.

title: Ikoyi Luxury Homes Surpass ₦1.1 Billion as Lagos Property Values Reach New High
description: Ikoyi Luxury Homes Surpass ₦1.1 Billion as Lagos Property Values Reach New High  nigeriahousingmarket.com
content: Ikoyi Luxury Homes Surpass ₦1.1 Billion as Lagos Property Values Reach New High housinglocal news Jul 10 Written By Ayomide Fiyinfunoluwa Luxury Real Estate in Ikoyi Reaches Record Prices Amid Strong Investor Demand Nigeria's premium residential market has reached another milestone, with the average selling price of five-bedroom homes in Ikoyi exceeding ₦1.1 billion, reinforcing the area's position as one of Africa's most valuable luxury real estate markets. The latest market analysis indicates sustained demand for high-end residential properties despite prevailing macroeconomic pressures, underscoring investor confidence in prime Lagos locations. / You Might Also Like / The 2026 Dangote Refinery Blueprint: Location, IPO, and Global Energy Impact The Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway: Transforming Nigeria’s Infrastructure Landscape Nigeria’s Housing Deficit: How Big Is the Problem in 2026? Mistakes First-Time Property Investors Make in Nigeria The findings reflect continued appreciation in property values across Ikoyi, where limited land supply, premium developments and strong demand from high-net-worth individuals continue to support price growth. Luxury Property Market Continues Upward Trend According to the latest residential market analysis reported by Nairametrics, average prices for five-bedroom detached homes in Ikoyi have crossed the ₦1.1 billion threshold, marking a significant milestone for Nigeria's luxury housing segment. The report attributes the sustained growth to strong demand for premium residential assets, rising construction costs, inflationary pressures and limited availability of prime land within the district. Ikoyi has consistently remained one of Lagos' most sought-after residential locations, attracting corporate executives, expatriates, diplomats, investors and high-net-worth individuals seeking exclusive residential developments. Limited Supply Supports Price Appreciation Property analysts note that one of the major drivers of Ikoyi's price growth is the scarcity of developable land. With few large parcels remaining for new developments, competition for prime sites has intensified, pushing both land and residential property values higher. The trend has been reinforced by the increasing cost of construction materials, financing and infrastructure development, all of which have contributed to higher development costs that are ultimately reflected in selling prices. Developers have also continued to invest in ultra-premium residential projects featuring smart home technologies, enhanced security systems, wellness facilities and luxury amenities designed to attract affluent buyers. Investor Confidence Remains Strong Despite broader economic challenges, the luxury residential segment has demonstrated resilience compared with other areas of the property market. Industry experts say many investors continue to view prime Lagos real estate as a long-term store of value, particularly during periods of inflation and currency volatilit
summary: The article reports that luxury homes in Ikoyi have surpassed ₦1.1 billion in value, driven by strong demand and limited land supply. This trend reinforces Ikoyi's position as one of Africa's most valuable luxury real estate markets. For Lagos real estate, this indicates sustained demand for high-end properties despite macroeconomic pressures.

title: The Horizon Estates Development That Challenged the Idea That Lekki Homes Must Be Expensive
description: The Horizon Estates Development That Challenged the Idea That Lekki Homes Must Be Expensive  Vanguard News
content: Horizon Heights, Ikate Lekki. By: Scheme Agatha Happiness The debate over housing affordability in Lagos has intensified in recent years, as rents continue to outpace incomes, particularly in high-demand neighborhoods such as Lekki Phase 1. Reports by BusinessDay, citing the Lagos Residential Market Report 2025, attribute the surge to a housing deficit of about 3.4 million units, rapid population growth and years of demand outstripping supply. Against that backdrop, calls for government intervention have grown louder. Yet before policymakers conclude that regulation alone is the answer, it is worth asking whether the problem stems from market failure itself or from longstanding supply constraints, and whether different development choices could improve affordability without distorting the market. Pearl Heights, Oniru, Victoria Island. It is known that developers have spent years perfecting the unclear logic of building regular homes, charging more, and calling it luxury. Horizon Estates made a different calculation with their developments, and Horizon Heights is one of the most visible pieces of evidence. Five hundred units on Palmspring Road, Lekki, delivered in 2022, were priced more than 20% below what comparable developments on the same axis charged. Not in a cheaper location or with inferior finishes, but in one of the most sought-after addresses in Lagos, at a price point the market had collectively deemed incompatible with that axis. For the executives, entrepreneurs, and diaspora returnees who bought there, people who had watched Lekki Phase 1 drift steadily beyond their reach, the development was less a real estate transaction than a correction. A signal that the gap between what they could afford and what the address demanded was not a law of nature. It was a developer’s choice. “We believe that homes should be priced in a way that people can live in them, and not just to beautify the skyline or the streets. That is why we work hard to beat down prices and guarantee affordability for our customers. We want to continue to enable homeownership by providing great quality homes at affordable prices like we have been doing for over a decade now, says Dr Ifeanyi Nduka, COO, Lekki Gardens Estate, owners of Horizon Estates Limited. The same pattern can be observed across the Horizon Estates portfolio. Cyberville, located by Lekki Phase 1 and completed in 2021, was developed as a contemporary urban community where residential comfort meets investment performance. Buyers who entered the project at N20.99 million in 2019 reportedly saw the value of their two-bedroom apartments rise to about N55 million within three years while earning roughly N7 million annually in rental income. Nearby, Horizonville in Ikate extends that philosophy into another rapidly expanding section of Lekki. Positioned close to the Third Roundabout and Christ Embassy Church, the development benefits from immediate access to one of Lagos’ busiest commercial corridors while ma
summary: The article reports on Horizon Estates' development, Horizon Heights, in Lekki Phase 1, which offered 500 units priced 20% below comparable developments in the same area. This challenges the notion that homes in Lekki must be expensive. The development implies that market forces can drive affordability without government intervention. For Lagos real estate, this suggests a potential shift towards more affordable housing options, particularly in high-demand areas.
trending_topics: affordable housing,market forces,developer strategies

title: Military mustn’t be used for land grabbing, CSOs tell FG
description: Military mustn’t be used for land grabbing, CSOs tell FG  Vanguard News
content: By Luminous Jannamike, Abuja ABUJA – A coalition of civil society organisations on Friday urged the Federal Government to keep security institutions out of an ownership dispute involving a parcel of land they said belongs to EFAB Properties, insisting that the matter should be resolved through the courts. To the coalition, the dispute goes beyond ownership of a piece of land. It said the real concern is safeguarding the integrity of public institutions and ensuring that matters already before the courts are resolved through due process rather than force. Speaking for the coalition at a press conference in Abuja, The Mighty One Empowerment Foundation founder Prof Barika Dominic Saro-Laka cautioned that security forces involvement in unsettled land disputes could weaken the public’s faith in the rule of law. “The military and its affiliates must not be used as tools for land grabbing, intimidation, and the enforcement of illegal contracts born out of fraud,” Saro-Laka said. The coalition maintained that the ownership dispute is already before the courts and urged all parties to allow the judicial process to take its course without interference. It specifically appealed to President Bola Tinubu, the Minister of Defence, the National Security Adviser and the Chief of Army Staff to ensure that no government institution is deployed in a manner that could undermine confidence in the administration of justice. ExecutiveBranch “We urge these authorities to intervene immediately and ensure that the rule of law prevails while the dispute is resolved through due process,” Saro-Laka said. Beyond the appeal to government, the coalition advised members of the public and prospective investors to exercise caution over any transactions involving the disputed property until the ownership issues are conclusively determined. “We advise the general public, investors, and stakeholders to exercise caution regarding any dealings relating to this property pending the resolution of the dispute,” he said. The coalition said it would continue to pursue lawful means to protect what it described as legitimate property rights, insisting that allowing the courts to determine the dispute without interference remained the best way to preserve public confidence in the rule of law. Comments expressed here do not reflect the opinions of Vanguard newspapers or any employee thereof.
summary: A coalition of civil society organizations in Abuja has urged the Federal Government to keep security institutions out of an ownership dispute involving a parcel of land. This means that the Lagos real estate market may benefit from a judicial resolution of disputes, potentially reducing the risk of land grabbing and intimidation. The article does not mention Lagos specifically, but the broader implications for Nigeria's property market are relevant to Mixta's operations.

title: Efab Reaffirms Ownership Claim Over Disputed Abuja Property
description: Efab Reaffirms Ownership Claim Over Disputed Abuja Property  LEADERSHIP Newspapers
content: Efab Properties has reaffirmed its claim of ownership over a disputed parcel of land in Abuja, with supporters urging the Federal Government and relevant authorities to ensure that the matter is resolved through due process. The position was highlighted at a press conference in Abuja organised by The Mighty One Empowerment Foundation and a coalition of civil society organisations. The groups said the land belongs to Efab Properties and called for the protection of what they described as the company’s legitimate property rights. Speaking at the event, the Founder of The Mighty One Empowerment Foundation, Professor Barika Saro-Laka, said Efab Properties remains the rightful owner of the land and appealed to the relevant authorities to safeguard the property while all outstanding issues are resolved through lawful procedures. The coalition urged President Bola Tinubu, the Minister of Defence, the National Security Adviser and the Chief of Army Staff to ensure that the dispute is handled in accordance with the rule of law and established legal processes. The groups also advised prospective investors and members of the public to exercise caution and verify all relevant documentation before entering into any transaction relating to the property until the ownership issues are fully resolved. According to the coalition, its intervention is aimed at supporting the protection of Efab Properties’ ownership claim and encouraging a peaceful and lawful resolution of the dispute. title: 2 Die As Flood Submerges Homes, Displaces Residents In Akwa Ibom
description: 2 Die As Flood Submerges Homes, Displaces Residents In Akwa Ibom  LEADERSHIP Newspapers
content: At least two persons have died after two days of torrential rainfall triggered devastating floods that submerged homes, displaced residents and crippled movement across Uyo, the Akwa Ibom State capital, and parts of Itu Local Government Area. The victims were reportedly swept away by floodwaters along Nkemba Street as heavy rains, which began early Thursday and continued into Friday, overwhelmed drainage channels and inundated major roads and residential communities. The flooding disrupted traffic on several roads, including Abak Road, Nkemba, Ukana Offot, Obio Offot, Use Offot, Udoette, Ebong Esien, Port Harcourt Street, Itam, Tabernacle Road and the Urua Ekpa axis of Itam in Itu Local Government Area. Several homes were submerged, forcing residents to flee, while vehicles were stranded as many roads became impassable. The palace of a traditional ruler was also flooded, with household property destroyed and many residents rendered homeless around the Port Harcourt Street axis of Uyo. A church building housing the Uyo branch of the Church of Christ on Abak Road and the popular Mechanics Village were also inundated, forcing worshippers to flee while traders counted heavy losses. A trader at the Mechanics Village, Udo John Udobot, lamented that repeated appeals for relocation had yielded no results. “We have been asking the government to relocate us. We were promised a new site years ago, but nothing has happened. Now we have lost goods to flooding and thieves who broke into our shops during the rain,” he said. Other badly affected areas included Two Lanes, Four Lanes, Aka Itiam, Ikpa Road, Shelter Afrique, IBB Avenue and surrounding communities, where floodwaters forced many residents to abandon their homes. Residents also reported that strong winds accompanying the rainfall damaged buildings and farmlands in parts of Eket Local Government Area, particularly in the Okon community. “This is too much for us. We need the state government and relevant agencies to come to our aid,” a resident of Eket, Nseobong Etim, appealed. At Tabernacle Road, residents struggled to rescue children and salvage household belongings as floodwaters swept through homes. RELATED NEWS Saving Sardauna’s Troubled House Varsity Faults NANS Over NELFUND Refund Claims Niger Delta, Place Of Special Concern To FG – Minister The situation sparked protests, with angry residents blocking parts of Ikot Ekpene Road and accusing the government and the contractor handling the ongoing Tabernacle Road project of failing to address the area’s persistent flooding. The protesters alleged that delays in completing the 2.2-kilometre road project had worsened flooding in the community. Responding, the Akwa Ibom State Government said Governor Umo Eno had directed the State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA), the Ministry of Environment and Mineral Resources, the Akwa Ibom State Waste Management and Environmental Protection Agency (AKSEPWMA) and other relevant agencies to commence emergency respo
summary: The article reports on devastating floods in Uyo, Akwa Ibom State, resulting in 2 deaths and displacement of residents, which has no direct impact on the Lagos real estate market, but highlights the importance of drainage and flood control measures in urban planning, a consideration for Mixta's projects in Lagos.
trending_topics: flood control, urban planning, infrastructure development

title: Federal Govt Targets 20,000 Homes Yearly As Germany Backs Nigeria’s Housing Drive
description: Federal Govt Targets 20,000 Homes Yearly As Germany Backs Nigeria’s Housing Drive  LEADERSHIP Newspapers
content: The federal government has disclosed plans to deliver 20,000 housing units annually, with about 15,000 homes already at various stages of completion nationwide, as it deepens partnership with the German Agency for International Development (GIZ) to accelerate affordable housing and urban renewal across the country. The minister of Housing and Urban Development, Muttaqha Rabe Darma, made the disclosure on Friday in Abuja during a meeting with a German delegation led by the Head of Cooperation at the Embassy of the Federal Republic of Germany, Dr Karin Jansen. The meeting reaffirmed both countries’ commitment to strengthening collaboration on affordable housing, sustainable urban development, climate-resilient infrastructure and inclusive communities under President Bola Ahmed Tinubu’s Renewed Hope Agenda. Darma said the federal government remained committed to addressing Nigeria’s huge housing deficit through strategic partnerships, noting that affordable housing was central to improving citizens’ welfare and stimulating economic growth. “We need to partner with GIZ to actualise our mandate. We are committed to delivering on the Renewed Hope Agenda, and we believe this partnership will strengthen our capacity to provide affordable housing, promote sustainable urban development and improve the quality of life of Nigerians,” the minister said. He acknowledged that although estimates of Nigeria’s housing deficit vary, the demand for decent and affordable homes remains enormous, with millions of Nigerians still lacking adequate shelter. RELATED NEWS Lagos Govt Seeks Student-led Solutions To Energy Challenges With New Competition Stock Market Records N9.342trn Week-on-week Gain USTDA Deploys US LNG Technology In Nigeria According to him, the ministry is also finalising a Social Housing Scheme that will extend affordable housing opportunities to all 774 local government areas, targeting low-income and vulnerable groups, including teachers, primary healthcare workers, persons with disabilities, young families and Nigerians in the diaspora seeking affordable homes. In another major intervention, Darma revealed that the federal government would commence the transformation of at least three major urban slums before the end of 2026 as part of a broader urban regeneration programme aimed at creating safer, more resilient and economically vibrant cities. “The future of our cities depends on deliberate planning, sustainable infrastructure and inclusive development. Beyond constructing houses, we are building communities where people can live with dignity, access essential services and enjoy a better quality of life,” he said. Speaking during the meeting, Dr Jansen described the engagement as timely, coming shortly after the recent Germany-Nigeria Government Negotiations on Development Cooperation, where both countries agreed to deepen collaboration in key sectors. She said Germany was prepared to expand cooperation with Nigeria in sustainable urban development,
summary: The federal government plans to deliver 20,000 housing units annually, with 15,000 homes already at various stages of completion nationwide. This indicates a significant push for affordable housing in Nigeria, which could create new demand for developers like Mixta Africa in the Lagos market. The partnership with the German Agency for International Development (GIZ) may also lead to increased investment in sustainable urban development and climate-resilient infrastructure.

title: CATA not your property, CONUA hits back at ASUU
description: CATA not your property, CONUA hits back at ASUU  Tribune Online
content: Add as a preferred source The Congress of University Academics (CONUA) has declared that the Consolidated Academic Tools Allowance (CATA) is a government-funded entitlement for all eligible academics, rejecting claims that it belongs exclusively to the Academic Staff Union of Universities (ASUU). Responding to allegations of “reaping where they did not sow,” CONUA argued that union pluralism is a legal reality in Nigeria. The union maintained that academic entitlements cannot be restricted based on union affiliation. The rebuttal was contained in a statement signed by CONUA National President, Dr ‘Niyi Sunmonu, on Thursday. The statement reacted to a report by the Nigerian Tribune Online, credited to ASUU President, Prof. Christopher Piwuna, regarding the implementation of CATA. The union pointed out that although it would not ordinarily have joined issues with ASUU, it did so to “correct a number of misconceptions capable of misleading” the public. “It should be clear to ASUU that CATA is not paid by ASUU,” the statement read. “It is a public employment entitlement funded by the Federal Government and administered through university managements in accordance with government directives.” ALSO READ: I’ll make sure every Nigerian feels the impact of my reforms — Tinubu CONUA noted that the Minister of Education, Dr Tunji Alausa, in a directive dated 9 June 2026 to Pro-Chancellors and Chairmen of Governing Councils, ordered the release of accumulated CATA funds — covering Professorial and Earned Academic Allowances — to all eligible academic staff on the CONUASS salary scale. The union emphasised that the directive does not restrict payment to any specific union. CONUA also dismissed claims that it relies on ASUU’s negotiations, revealing that it has concluded its own renegotiation deal with the Federal Government and is currently awaiting the formal signing of the agreement. The union criticised ASUU’s use of derogatory terms like “shameless” and “cheap publicity,” urging a shift toward reasoned discourse. “The future of Nigerian university unionism lies not in monopoly but in competition of ideas, mutual respect, and fidelity to the rule of law,” CONUA concluded. title: Dariann Court Handover Marks Another Delivery Milestone for MKH Properties
description: Dariann Court Handover Marks Another Delivery Milestone for MKH Properties  Punch Newspapers
content: Dariann Court Handover Marks Another Delivery Milestone for MKH Properties July 10, 2026 1:52 pm Branded Content Kindly share this story: MKH Properties has completed the allocation and formal handover of units at Dariann Court, its premium residential development in Sangotedo, Lagos, with a few units remaining available for immediate purchase. The handover ceremony, held on May 21, 2026, at the mini estate within Olive Park Estate, on the Lekki-Epe Expressway, brought together the company’s senior leadership, new property owners, real estate partners, and representatives from Keystone Bank. For the buyers who received their allocation documents and keys that afternoon, it marked the end of a purchase process and the beginning of a home they could move into the same day. Dr MuibiKehinde Hammed, CEO of MKH Properties, was unambiguous about what buyers were receiving. “Dariann Court is a mini estate with 4-bedroom duplexes and 4-bedroom terraces. Everything that makes a home look good in terms of technology is provided here. It is a complete project. You can pay now and move in immediately,” he told guests at the ceremony. The development features fingerprint access control, 24-hour CCTV surveillance, motion-detecting security lighting, and a fully gated perimeter, all of which are installed and operational. There are no outstanding construction phases, pending infrastructure, or waiting period for buyers who proceed today. TayoBolarinde, Managing Director of MKH Properties, led the formal welcome at the ceremony, with Executive Director Commercial IdowuOlukunle and Executive Director Operations Deborah Oladipupo also in attendance. The presence of the company’s full executive leadership at a handover event reflects the level of priority MKH Properties places on delivery as a public commitment rather than an internal milestone. Keystone Bank, which served as a financial partner on the project, was represented at the ceremony by its Regional Head for Victoria Island, DrOlufunkeOyero-Nwanze. “We are here not just to commission an estate but to celebrate a very powerful partnership between MKH Properties and Keystone Bank,” she said. The bank’s active involvement in the project from development through to handover reflects the level of institutional confidence Dariann Court attracted from the outset. title: Abia Firms Reopen Five Production Lines, Otti Launches Housing Estates
description: Boniface Okoro in Umuahia The Abia State government says the revived Enyimba Automated Shoe Company and the Enyimba Garments Factory both in Aba have established five production lines for the two
content:
Boniface Okoro in Umuahia

The Abia State government says the revived Enyimba Automated Shoe Company and the Enyimba Garments Factory both in Aba have established five production lines for the two factories which are also developing new brand lines for their products. 

Commissioner for Information, Prince Okey Kanu, announced this at Government House Umuahia, while addressing the press on the outcome of this week’s State Executive Council meeting presided over by Governor Alex Otti.

He said that both firms have been restored to active production after years of decline, attributing the revival of the facilities to reforms initiated by the Governor Otti-led administration and the appointment of Ms Nena Kalu-Ogba to oversee the companies in 2025.

According to the commissioner, the revival of the company reflects the present administration’s commitment to industrialisation, job creation and sustainable economic growth.

“To date, they have been able to establish five production lines for both factories, and the new management is going ahead to develop new brand lines for their products that are capable of competing in international markets,” Kanu said.

He also announced that the state government would soon flag off the Pocket Layout Industrial Estate project, and said that the initiative is designed to provide functional and affordable housing for residents.

He said the procurement process for the project has reached an advanced stage, with bids from prospective contractors already opened and the list of successful bidders expected to be published very soon.

“The Pocket-Layout Industrial Estate project is due for a flag off. With the opening of bids from prospective contractors to the project, a comprehensive list of successful contractors will be published within the next one week or less,” he said. 

He explained that the project comprises two components, including the Old Civil Service Commission premises and the defunct Abia Hotels premises, adding that it was currently being cleared to enable construction works to begin.

Contributing, the Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director of Enyimba Automated Shoe Company Limited and Enyimba Garment Factory Limited, Ms. Kalu-Ogba, said the state government had successfully revived the two factories through the acquisition of modern production and finishing equipment to enable them compete with global brands.

“What we’ve done is to revive the factories, the two factories. And we have purchased new machines for finishing, especially, so that we can compete favourably with global products. So we have all the machines. 

“The governor has been gracious enough to provide the funds to purchase new machinery,” she stated. 

According to the CEO, the intervention was aimed at changing the long-held perception that Aba-made products were of inferior quality, noting that the factories have been rehabilitated into conducive production environments, while workers’ welfare has improved with regular salary payments.

She added that facilities at the factory were now well positioned to produce high-quality footwear and garments capable of meeting international standards, maintaining that the government has resolved to ensure the firms deliver.
summary: The Abia State government reports that two factories, Enyimba Automated Shoe Company and Enyimba Garments Factory, have reopened with 5 production lines established, and a new housing estate project, Pocket Layout Industrial Estate, is set to be flagged off. This development suggests a positive trend in industrialization and housing in Nigeria, which could have a ripple effect on the Lagos real estate market, particularly in the affordable housing segment. The article highlights the state government's commitment to job creation and sustainable economic growth.
trending_topics: industrialization,housing,Abia State,Governor Alex Otti

title: How mechanics, youths make money from Lagos floods
description: Recently, the torrential rains that flooded Nigeria’s commercial capital and brought disaster on residents, also brought relief to the sweltering heat in the city, as well as introduced business opportunities that mitigated losses. The flood left submerged homes behind, crippled businesses, damaged vehicles and introduced heartbreaking stories of lives disrupted in a matter of hours. […]
content:
Recently, the torrential rains that flooded Nigeria’s commercial capital and brought disaster on residents, also brought relief to the sweltering heat in the city, as well as introduced business opportunities that mitigated losses.

The flood left submerged homes behind, crippled businesses, damaged vehicles and introduced heartbreaking stories of lives disrupted in a matter of hours. However, in the moment of chaos and loss, business opportunities emerged and many victims found consolation.

As floodwaters swept through major roads and neighborhoods, from Victoria Island, Lekki to Gbagada, Mushin, Isashi, Ijanikin, Ojo, Ajegunle, Egbeda, Agege and Okota, the city wore two contrasting faces.

While thousands mourned the destruction of property acquired over the years, others seized the moment to earn income.

The morning of last Sunday began like every other rainy day. Dark clouds gathered over Lagos before a torrential downpour turned streets into rivers and highways into parking lots. Within hours, vehicles were trapped in rising waters, homes were inundated and commercial activities came to a standstill.

 

Youths take advantage

Meanwhile, while tears flowed, dreams shattered, thousands counted their losses, others found opportunity in adversity and in the midst of the pains of others.

At major flood flashpoints, groups of young men gathered with wheelbarrows, wooden planks and ropes, offering rescue services to stranded motorists and earning a living.

“We started early because we knew that cars would get stuck. We charged between N5,000 and N20,000, depending on the size of the vehicle,” one of the vehicle pushers said with a grin.

“Desperate motorists who were unable to restart their vehicles willingly paid to have them pushed to dry ground. We were not cheating anyone.

“We were providing service. Without us, many cars would still be stranded,” Ibrahim, a cart pusher insisted.

At the Trade Fair area of Ojo, many teenagers carried traders across the flooded Mile 2-Badagry expressway on their backs, ensuring they reached dry land without soaking their clothes.

“Some paid N500, others N1,000. It may not be much but it helped us feed to ourselves,” one of the teenagers rendering service said.

Commercial tricycle and motorcycle operators also enjoyed brisk business as transport fares surged on routes still accessible despite the flooding.

Roadside food vendors, sachet water sellers, umbrella hawkers and raincoat merchants equally recorded increased patronage from commuters trapped in an endless traffic.

Also, vehicle mechanics worked almost without rest. “Our workshop is full. Many drivers ignored the warnings and entered deep water. Some engines will require complete replacement,” a mechanic in Oshodi said while inspecting another flood-damaged engine.

Tow truck operators also reported one of their busiest days in months, moving stalled vehicles from flooded roads to repair workshops across the city.

Even those benefitting financially admitted that the situation was bittersweet.

“We don’t pray for disasters, but when floods happen, it is our opportunity to earn a living. Tomorrow there may be no work again,” a wheelbarrow operator said.

‘Flood comes with work’ Interestingly, for a group of street urchins and unemployed youths, the floods however, marks the beginning of an unexpected business season.

At flood-prone spots along Ago Palace Way, Lekki-Epe Expressway, Gbagada, Oshodi, Egbeda, Ajegunle and Maryland, dozens of young men wait patiently for the rains to intensify.

Armed with nothing than their physical strength, they spring into action when vehicles become stranded in floodwaters.

“Once the rain starts, we know work has come,” said 22-year-old Sefiu who claimed to have spent the last three rainy seasons pushing broken-down vehicles out of flooded roads.

“On a good day, I make between N1,000 and N5,000, but on flood days, I earn over N15,000 before nightfall.”

For him, charges depend on the size of the vehicle, the depth of the flood and the distance pushed.

“For small cars we collect between N5,000 and N10,000. SUVs cost between N10,000 and N20,000 because they are heavier. Sometimes we work as a team of four or five, and share the money.”

Nearby, 19-year-old Bodunde said flood days often bring in more money than weeks of casual labour.

“There are days I go home with N20,000 after sharing with my friends. On normal days, I struggle to make N3,000 or N5,000 doing odd jobs.”

Another youth, who identified himself simply as Mojeed, described the work as risky but worthwhile.

“We enter dirty floodwater for hours. Sometimes there are broken bottles, sharp metals or open drains hidden beneath the water. But we have no choice. This is how we survive.”

Some motorists initially complain about the charges, he admitted, but desperation often changes their minds.

“When a car is trapped in the flood and water is entering the engine, one doesn’t have much options. It’s either you wait for a tow truck that may take hours or you pay us to push it to safety.”

“We help commercial buses, trucks and even delivery vans,” another youth added. “Anybody whose vehicle gets stuck is our customer.”

The boom extends beyond vehicle pushers. Teenagers earn money carrying commuters across flooded roads on their backs or helping them navigate submerged walkways.

“We charge between N500 and N1,500 per person depending on the water level,” said one of the youths. “Office workers don’t want to arrive at work soaked.”

While their earnings offer temporary relief from unemployment, many acknowledged the irony of profiting from another person’s pains.

“We don’t pray for floods,” Seyi said quietly. “People lose property and spend money fixing their cars. But when the floods come, this is our opportunity to feed ourselves. If there are no floods, there is no business for us.”

“Once the rain is heavy, we know that today will be a good day,” said 24-year-old Wasiu, a battery charger at Mafoluku-Oshodi.

He explained that it becomes difficult for vehicles to start once flood water enters the engine.

“On an a day when there is no rain, I don’t make more than N2,000 or N3,000. But on flood days, I can go home with between N20,000 and M25,000” he added.

Speaking further Mojeed said every flood creates its own economy.

“The deeper the water, the more vehicles get trapped. Some drivers think they can cross, but halfway through the engine goes off. That’s when they begin looking for us.”

Standing beside a flooded section of the road at Tolu area of Ajegunle, another youth, Kunle, said they often work in teams because of the weight of some vehicles.

“Our targets are usually “tokumbo vehicles coming from the Tin-Can Port.

“For a small saloon car, we charge between N5,000 and N10,000. Jeeps cost between N15,000 and N25,000 because they are heavier. If the owner is in a hurry, he doesn’t argue too much.”

Twenty-year-old Chinedu described the work as physically exhausting.

“Sometimes we push vehicles for almost one kilometre before reaching dry ground. By evening, your hands and legs are aching. But when you remember there is money waiting, you keep going.”

He said they face dangers that most people never notice.

“There are open gutters hidden inside the floodwater,” one of them explained.

“You can suddenly step into one and disappear. We have also been injured by broken bottles, sharp metals and other objects washed into the water.”

Despite the risks, they return every time heavy rain falls.

“If we hear on the radio that there will be heavy rain, we already know where to position ourselves,” another youth admitted with a smile.

Beyond pushing vehicles, the flood also creates other income streams.

Some of the youths carry commuters on their backs or escort them through submerged streets.

Asked how they feel making money from other people’s hardship, the group fell silent for a moment.

“We don’t celebrate anybody’s loss,” Chinedu finally replied.

“We know what it means to struggle because we are struggling too. We simply take advantage of the opportunity to earn an honest living. If there were jobs for us, many of us wouldn’t be standing in floodwater all day.”

As another dark cloud gathered over the Lagos skyline, the youths looked hopefully toward the road, scanning for the next stranded vehicle.

For them, every heavy rainfall is more than a weather event. It is a rare chance to earn enough in a single day to feed, pay rent or support younger siblings.

In a city where survival often depends on seizing every opportunity, floodwaters have become an unlikely source of livelihood, even as they continue to bring heartbreak to thousands of other Lagos residents.

 

The devastation

For residents of Isashi in Iba Local Council Development Area, survival became the only priority.

Mrs Felicia Mba, a mother of four whose bungalow was submerged by floodwater said, “We didn’t think it would get this bad. We first noticed water coming into the sitting room; and before long, every room was flooded. We tried to move our television, refrigerator and mattresses but the water rose too quickly. We left with only our children and few bags.”

When the flood finally receded, it left mud, debris behind and shattered hopes.

“Our furniture is damaged. Our children’s books are gone. Even important documents were soaked. We don’t know where to from,” one of the victims said.

The same story echoed across several flood-prone communities.

In Mushin, traders watched helplessly as floodwater invaded their shops, destroying food items, clothing and electronics.

“I just received fresh supplies two days ago. Everything is soaked. I borrowed money to stock this shop. I honestly don’t know how I will recover,” Matthew Igwe, a shop owner in the area lamented while separating damaged cartons from those that could still be salvaged.

For motorists, the flood became an expensive lesson. Along Ago Palace Way in Okota, the Lekki-Epe expressway, Ikorodu Road and parts of the Apapa-Oshodi expressway, dozens of vehicles stalled after drivers attempted to navigate flooded roads.

“My mechanic said water entered the engine. The repair will cost almost what I earn in several months. I was only trying to get to work,” one frustrated car owner said while waiting beside his disabled vehicle along the Charlie Boy area of Gbagada.

Across Gbagada, Maryland, Ojota and Surulere, office workers abandoned buses and walked through knee-deep water after public transportation became scarce.

Also, many businesses opened late as employees struggled to reach their workplaces.

Restaurant owners, operators of pharmacies and supermarkets reported heavy losses after customers stayed away throughout the day.

Chinyere Nwachukwu, who owns a restaurant at Aguda said: “We also had to throw away perishable food because there was no electricity for several hours. So it wasn’t just the flood; it was a complete loss of business.”

Parents described the emotional toll of seeing years of sacrifice washed away.

Fadipe Ojo said, “We are already battling inflation. School fees are due. Food prices keep rising. Now, flood has destroyed our furniture and electrical appliances. It is difficult to remain hopeful.”

LG  chairman’s visit to flood affected  communities

Meanwhile, the Executive Chairman of Olorunda Local Council Development Area, Hon. Ajose Peter Kumayon, recently, visited the flood-ravaged Obele-Esepe and Ibiye communities to assess the extent of damage caused by the recent torrential rainfall and fast-track emergency intervention for affected residents. The visit was aimed at determining the immediate palliative measures needed to restore movement while permanent solutions are being planned.

Speaking during the inspection, Ajose said he deemed it necessary to personally assess the situation and ensure urgent action to ease the hardship residents are facing.

He disclosed that temporary palliative works would commence immediately to make the roads accessible during the rainy season, while a comprehensive reconstruction of the drainage system would be undertaken after the rains as a lasting solution.

The flooding has left both communities largely cut off from the Lagos-Badagry Expressway. In Obele-Esepe, residents have resorted to using canoes, paying transport fares to cross to the Ile-Epo area of Araromi-Ale due to the inundated access road.

Ajose assured residents that his administration, in collaboration with the Lagos State Government, remains committed to delivering both immediate relief and permanent infrastructure that will prevent a recurrence of the flooding.

 

Govt’s efforts

Reacting to the flood situation, the Lagos State Government attributed the widespread flooding to unusually heavy rainfall, combined with high Atlantic Ocean and lagoon tides that slowed the discharge of storm water.

Officials also blamed blocked drainage channels, indiscriminate waste disposal and illegal construction on drainage setbacks for the worsening the situation.

Weekend Trust reports that Governor Babajide Sanwo-Olu has since approved the dredging of 28 additional primary drainage channels as part of efforts to improve flood management while emergency officials continue to monitor vulnerable communities.

The Nigeria Hydrological Services Agency has also warned residents of riverine communities, including Badagry, Epe, Ikorodu and Isheri to remain vigilant as the rainy season intensifies.

But for families now cleaning mud from their living rooms, drying photographs under the sun and calculating the cost of replacing destroyed household items, government’s assurances offer little immediate comfort.

Every damaged sofa, soaked school uniform and broken television tells the story of years of sacrifice erased in a single afternoon.

But amid the heartbreak, Lagosians have continued to display the resilience for which the city is known.

Our correspondent also reports that neighbours helped one another to rescue belongings and strangers pushed vehicles from floodwaters without expecting payment.

Volunteers directed traffic through submerged roads. Families also shared food and temporary shelter with those displaced.

The flood also exposed not only the weaknesses in urban infrastructure but the enduring strength of the human spirit.

In Lagos, where survival often demands resilience, one person’s tragedy becomes another’s livelihood.

Behind every flooded street lies a family trying to rebuild, a trader searching for fresh capital, a motorist facing unexpected repair bills and a child wondering why the rain carried away everything they called home.

As the clouds gather once again over Africa’s largest city, one question lingers in the minds of many residents: Will  Lagos ever be prepared for the rains?
summary: The article reports on how youths and mechanics in Lagos are making money from the recent floods by offering rescue services to stranded motorists, charging between N5,000 and N20,000 per vehicle. This highlights the resilience and adaptability of Lagos residents, but also underscores the city's infrastructure challenges. For the Lagos real estate market, this means that developers like Mixta Africa need to consider flood resilience and mitigation strategies in their projects.
trending_topics: flood resilience, infrastructure development, informal economy

mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Mixta Africa's projects, such as Lakowe Crossings and Lagos New Town, may need to incorporate flood mitigation measures to enhance their attractiveness and value to potential buyers
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta Africa could explore opportunities to develop flood-resilient infrastructure and amenities in their projects, creating a unique selling point and competitive advantage
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: Failure to address flood resilience and infrastructure challenges could pose a risk to the successful execution and marketability of Mixta Africa's projects

title: PFIPC saga: Whither the National Assembly?
description: The current administration of President Bola Tinubu is presently reeling from a broadside salvo of a scandal that questions its handling of a phantom agency that, though not created by law, has been operating illegally and enjoys the official perquisites of office. The perquisites include the allocation to the agency an office space and equipment […]
content:
The current administration of President Bola Tinubu is presently reeling from a broadside salvo of a scandal that questions its handling of a phantom agency that, though not created by law, has been operating illegally and enjoys the official perquisites of office. The perquisites include the allocation to the agency an office space and equipment in the Federal Secretariat complex, Abuja, assignment of support staff, as well as inclusion in the 2026 federal government budget as a bona fide, budget-funded statutory agency, whereby it was allocated a whopping sum of N1.3 billion.

Added to the foregoing perquisites, its leadership enjoyed access to sensitive, top-level governance circles. That is the saga of the so-called Presidential Foreign Investment Promotion Commission (PFIPC), which has been operating for some time under one Adeyemi Adeniyi Matthew, who had been parading himself as its director-general.

In the play-out of the saga, which came to light through red flag by Femi Gbajabiamila, the Chief of Staff to President Bola Tinubu in a letter last year, courtesy of a protest by the extant Nigerian Investment Promotion Commission (NIPC), arose an allegation by Adeyemi Adeniyi Matthew that his dud outfit came to be by a collaboration between him and the former, albeit through a proxy, and to whom he had paid the whopping sum of N400 million out of an earlier agreed bribe of N600 million, leaving an outstanding balance of N200 million.

Typical of such a high-brow scam, its tentacles have also linked George Akume, the Secretary to the Federal Government, from whom all appointments and placements of top public officers flow. Also in the tale are a complement of yet unnamed top government officials whose acts of commission and omission must have enabled the manifestation of the outrage of the PFIPC.

Meanwhile, as at the last count, the saga has attracted reactions from top-ranking quarters that accentuate its profundity as a high point in the daily degrading culture and processes of governance, as well as the rape of the common weal of the country.

In one vein are the reactions that indict top officials of the administration as complicit in the evil enterprise. In another vein are reactions that vindicate the chief of staff to the president as not complicit in the enterprise, as the culprit could as well be a lone wolf out to drop the name of Gbajabiamila for self-serving reasons.

However, topical among the reactions is the directive of the president to the Independent Corrupt Practices Commission (ICPC) to carry out a full investigation of the matter, pursuant to establishing who did what, when and how. This is even in yet another vein, the reaction that disqualifies the president from directing the ICPC to investigate the matter as such would amount to the Presidency probing itself. 

Just as well, public concern is also trending over how the National Assembly is viewing the entire saga since by virtue of the inclusion of the PFIPC in the 2026 budget, the association of the saga with the institution cannot be overlooked as less significant. Rather, the circumstance calls for a more discretionary intervention by the institution in the interest of safeguarding its internal processes of exercising power over the nation’s purse from such outrageous development as the PFIPC in the future.

Given the constitutional oversight role of the National Assembly, while the inclusion of an illegal agency in the 2026   national budget constitutes a grave offence by the executive arm, its escape from the scrutiny of the National Assembly remains a major indictment of the institution as well, which even outweighs the slip by the executive arm. That is why the reported stance of the Senate to await the outcome of the investigation by the ICPC before taking action remains an abdication of responsibility by the institution.

 By the extant provisions of the Nigerian constitution, responsibility for the integrity and health of the country’s fiscal processes is vested on the National Assembly based on the notion that whereas the operatives in the executive arm may falter in providing wholesome provisions in the budget, the legislature would serve as the gatekeeper to keep things in order. Hence, the present scenario of less-than-prompt commitment of the Senate to intervene constitutes a situation whereby the gatekeeper fails in its duty and provides room for the wolves to gain access and wreak havoc on the system.

In fact, taking a cue from the constitutional angle, along with the documented history of budget management and otherwise in the National Assembly, the association with and even inclusion of a fake PFIPC in the2026 budget provides enough justification for both chambers of the institution to adopt a more robust attitude towards auditing the internal processes of oversight of the various organs of the executive arm of government. To clarify from a personal angle, this author recalls vividly how during his days as the Director of Information and Publications in the National Assembly, he, with some subordinates, would go through the draft budget presented by the president to cross-match the entries with the schedule of MDAs, and even notify agencies whose entries were missing before printing the same for consideration by legislators.  

 Meanwhile, for recall, budget management by the National Assembly over the years has also not been without the annual rounds of rumours of illegal insertions by sundry interests in the name of ‘budget padding’. With the trending case of the PFIPC, the challenge of inserting unauthorised budget items may have been further heightened by the more brazen incidence of inclusion of illegal agencies in the budget.

The situation also raises the question of whether the PFIPC is the only agency so illegally inserted for budget funding, presently or even before. After all, this is Nigeria where snakes reportedly swallow public funds, and public officials in the custody of public funds store such largesse in toilet soak-away pits. In such a situation, the circumstance of the PFIPC fits into the format of high level guile.
summary: The article reports on a scandal involving the Presidential Foreign Investment Promotion Commission (PFIPC), a phantom agency that has been operating illegally and was allocated N1.3 billion in the 2026 federal government budget. This means that the lack of transparency and accountability in government may impact investor confidence in Lagos real estate, potentially affecting projects like Lakowe Crossings and Lakowe Annexe. The article does not provide specific details on the Lagos market.
trending_topics: government scandal, transparency, accountability, investor confidence
